So, another man from there, from that Roseburg country, had come up to Palouse and was running a stagecoach. From Dayton to Colfax to Palouse. And they were friends, and he wrote. Not drop out at school, what have you. So finally they sent her to the one here and every effort was made to keep that girl away from that part of the business when she came. But the sisters and I don't want to support her forever. She wanted her to go to work. And the girl didn't want to go to work, but finally a job was gotten to her as a waitress. She hadn't gone to school, so it was a decent place to work. It was in China and it was a good human, but he was rather nice to his health. I'll say that living in that place wasn't an ideal spot, but she could have burned enough and lived someplace else. There was a house where there was Oh sure, but they kept her very separate from us. So she had that time, but she wanted to take another afternoon off, so she asked her to go. So she went anyway, even though the boss said she couldn't, so she lost her job. That happened a time or two and after a while, she didn't have any place to go and pretty soon she was in another hotel, not where her sister was. She wouldn't let her. She knew, she thought, she said there wasn't anything else for me to do. Well, she had every chance. She just didn't want to. Everybody has had to do certain things. You have to go to work whether you want to or not. You don't stop and go. They take that off just because you want to. If you do, you have to face the consequences. You lose your job. And there was a time limit when her sister, who was a sporting woman, would not support her. So, she became one herself. But her sister really didn't want her to. No, she didn't. She made every effort to keep her from her. I would say she was very protective. And usually they are, if they have a child, usually they keep them in the boarding school, keep them way away and are very careful. Usually they, this is the usual thing. They didn't have a regular job ever. Never had a regular job. And I know all them. They have their classes and they look down upon each other. And there was one girl came and stayed at this house. She was pregnant. She was going to have a baby. And, um, she's had a room. Because they are hotels, you know. And, um, I remember one of the ladies said, I would like to adopt that baby. I like the mother. She's kind of nice. But she said, I wouldn't have his child. He said, he is dumb. Oh! She just thought he was terrible. He said he had different girls that he worked with. But he always, he had gotten this one. She was supposed to be going to college. And her father said, you're my different pal. And there she was. And she had gotten pregnant. And this was, she had been twice to, uh, Salvation Army and they wouldn't take them a third time. That way they draw the line after five, you know. They'll help a girl in trouble. Twice. Twice. With more severity. But about the third time, I don't say they wouldn't accept her, but, uh, there would be quite a lot of talk. Because they don't approve, you know.

And she would be at the hospital just when the baby came. And I know that this boy, this man, that she absolutely would not have that man's child. And, uh, of course, the arrangements are made for the baby to be adopted through the children's home. I knew then. We curled her hair. And, she got to the hospital. One of the sisters recognized her. Up there. She knew her father. He knew that he was a nice man. A decent family. He was a ruler. He was trying to do the best. He was paying all his money to keep his own copy. And so she went to Mother Superior and she said, they decided he should be told what his daughter was doing. So they did.

The type of corset to get, the size, the height. And she got so and so. She'd shop for all the people up the river, you know. And there was something when she had to, one of the men that came in, he was next door to the store, getting very drunk. And she was just that type, you know. And you did business with some of them, but that was all, you know. And, no, they were, they were around in the West in the early days. speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I mean, quite often they lived above a saloon or back of a saloon. In the early times, later they didn't, you know, in Lewiston, when I knew them, there were days when they had nothing to do, no direct connection, except maybe some of them looked like some.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But it would be the hotels, mostly. Yeah.

words: One fellow I know, college boy, went to Chicago for a convention of his fraternity. It was nationwide, and all the taxi drivers were asking if they wanted to see a girl. That type of thing. I mean, those things had been around, but, as one said, things they could at least lay off a little bit. There was an often low class drifted in for the CCCs at Pierce. When they came, and you know, they didn't have much money to spend, I think. Most of it went to their family, but they had just a little bit. And they were, of course, we've been in a terrible depression. Things were very hard, and all of a sudden, we had another very nice class with these people. We didn't know them, and drifting through one time, and where were they going, and they were going to Pierce. And we found out they were drifting up there to get these boys' money. And none of them looked young, I suppose they were, but I was young then. Of course, 30 of them were very old. And I thought, oh my goodness, there isn't much money either. And that was when they finally closed Pierce and made it on the balance at the time. I did the young head of customer. At that time, I remember this was depression used. And she was a very pleasant, nice young girl, and her brother had a oil company, franchising in Lewiston. He was a standard oil man. Worked for the company, rather. They were distributed.

English, German. And she's a son in Italian, but I wouldn't be as good with the economy.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: well, diplomatic corps, but I knew Lewiston and everything. She said, but I would be glad, Catherine, to work at Woolworths, anything, to have a job. Well, no house is really big enough for two women. She and her father were living with her brother and his wife. His wife was our customer and they were of different turn and people and terms. But they weren't exactly broke. She had enough to ever hear a fake, but they needed to be very much concerned.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And I thought, what do I do with somebody like this?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: So I sent her to T. Shur, who would be managing the chamber of commerce at the time, and I thought, poor T, she's got a lot of trouble. But I wonder if she'll speak to me the next time I see her, but she said, Catherine, that was the nicest thing you did. Send me that lovely girl. And I said, well, I didn't know what else to do. And she said, well, of course, I don't have a job either. But she's public stenographer, we're calling it that down there. There is a guy who wants a letter written. She was trying to introduce her to everybody in the chamber of commerce and she sent out a circular about her and things. Then the CCs came along right soon after that. And to qualify, you had to be without a job, you know, to work. But a lot of girls could qualify. Well, of course, she was without a job, so you can imagine she could take dictation from any of the other poor little stenographers they had around. And I didn't know, oh, they had terrific Army terms that we'd never heard of. I can understand how most of the girls were having a heck of a time with it. And here were all the officers lined up in this D .C. to get her their dictation and none of the others. So the colonel took a look and he said, she's my private secretary, enough choice. She'll work for me and my staff only. And she went along when they closed Pierce. And of course, I said, well, that must have become interesting while it was here. She said, oh, no, it isn't. It's hot weather here, but it's cold up there. Maybe I haven't brought a wrap. And he says, at 1030, order a car. We're going to Pierce. And all the way up there, he dictates real rapidly and the car is jollying like everything. And we get up there and he's mad. So he's walking with his taking yard long steps and I'm following him through the brush, which is tearing my nylons terribly and my sleepless dress. And he is so mad at what he has found, the conditions of these things, these people that followed these boys getting all mad. The Carpetbaggers would be one name. I don't know what you'd call camp followers or something. And they were a lousy bunch. And he's giving me this dictation and I'm trying to take it while he's walking through the brush. He said, Catherine, it isn't fun. I can imagine it. She was probably the only one that could handle it at all. And they closed Pierce. You know. They just made out of balance. They had to because so much of this had come in. And no, it wasn't just the Pierce people. It was the others. Yeah, these were hardened girls that came in from outside. speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, maybe a little bit more in that. But, they drank more right afterwards. And, they drink more now. It was served in... Of course, the WCTU was more of a thing in the 90's. But, it was served in nice homes. It was something to know what kind of wine, what kind of food. And, that's a sort of a gourmet fashion.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But, drunkenness was found out. And, well, it is now. And, then, they went out some in Prohibition times, through speakeasies and that sort of thing. But, I don't think women drank as much. So, I don't think it was as much drinking, actually, if people did not. It paid quite a price for rotten stuff, I guess.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And, um... But, to say that there was... Really, there wasn't as much drinking as is done. Here, at least, there were certain people that you could probably buy liquor from, and you probably knew of. I never thought of them much as gangsters. They were just... Local bootleggers. Yeah, not particularly a dangerous sinister type, just the bootlegger. And, it was only in the cities where they were... Had their fights, you know, their gang wars. We didn't have anything like that. There was nothing like that. Some officers were on the tape, and some weren't. And, they were... That was... Some were crooked. I think that was the most deplorable part of the Depression to us, was when we had an officer that was crooked. Of course, a rather humorous thing happened to my father, in that... He was riding the Camry in Giudetta, and Mr. Dustin, who owned it, and they were partners in the venture, was a strict teetotaler. Very, very strict. Very high morals, you know. Had a big vineyard back of his house, and I know one man later said, Oh, I never knew about Mr. Dustin, as he must have surely liked his wine, and I could hear him drag and drop. He was completely tilted. He said, Well, he had the grapes for it. I said, He enjoyed having the vineyard. Thought it was nice.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But, you'd gone there and said, Now, what grapes do I use in this kind of wine?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You probably wouldn't have left with any grapes at all, because he wouldn't have approved of it at all, and he would have no part in selling them to me. I said, Here's a drink. Among this background, and in a small town Camry, Mr. Giudetta, you hire everybody in the town, and this fellow worked for my father, and he was, um, um, we knew he was some kind of a character, but he behaved himself around there, and a man came and said he was the inspector. Well, Ken had inspectors. Dad thought it was a heck of a time to inspect, and we were almost through. They were just labeling, shipping out. They weren't really doing anything. They always made a few barrels of vinegar. It's a byproduct of apples. Sell a few barrels of it. He examined that. He thought very, very carefully.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: So what?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And after he was gone, he poked around a lot. Dad thought. Didn't know his business. Most of the inspectors didn't. It was about his feeling. Art said to my father, Geez, good thing I moved by still. And that's a plot. He said, I had my still down here under the camera. If Camry should have built up on the still, it's like, well, I have my word.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You had the still down there?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He said, yeah. Oh, he said, don't worry, Nellie. I haven't taken the blame. I said, you just don't know anything about the blame. There's been no way that that could have been done. He could just think about this, you know. If Camry had been found with a still, oh my word. And he said, well, he didn't know either. He said, no, no. But that would never do. My father was very strict. But now this wouldn't have been quite as upset about it. Oh, he would have been very unhappy. But Mr. Dusty, whoa. He said, oh, they had the still in the camera. He said, that ain't good as it was gone, because he didn't know anything about it. But he never occurred to, never occurred to this man as a prohibition. He came under the guy. He said he was. And it was gone. But oh boy. And anybody that knew my father would have known he wouldn't have had the still. That wouldn't have been his thing at all. Because he didn't have much respect for that kind of law -breaking. But he wasn't as personal as much of a teatope. You know. I mean, he thought a certain grade of whiskey was probably nice. He didn't overdo, and it might help a cold or something that way. It had definite medicinal values, maybe. And some of it was enjoyable, and he didn't get drunk. It was okay. But of course, his pardon didn't improve it at all. It was just simply beyond the pay alone. He was completely, it was the most morally improbable thing imaginable in a man's still. Oh boy. I'm sure he would have realized that my father didn't know about it, but oh my goodness. That would have been a, oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the cop that was on the take? What did he do?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He just took...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just, what did it mean? He let the bootleggers in each other?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yeah, they just paid him off.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They paid him off. And there were cops on the take, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Lewiston?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, in Lewiston. Never played that game. I think all over there were some. There were some that worked. There were some that worked. Yes, there were some that didn't. And got quite wealthy, some of them. Some of them just moderately. And occasionally there would be somebody from outside, some Denver people come through. Sometimes I think they took her, sometimes they didn't. And some people would be arrested. But usually they were the, not too respected people. They were, oh, chicken thieves and bootleggers. It was about the way people like my father did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, these cops?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, the people that were selling my company. The people that were selling my company. Not the best, you know. And of course, my father was a rather narrow -minded on that. He, more so than my mother, he kind of looked down on everybody's pencil. And yet, people might take a drink, but he just didn't think that was the best of occupations. Once in a while it was alright, but not too, really not too tough. But perfectly horrified, I didn't remember if his family wanted to. But he wouldn't have wanted to be a bootlegger. He just didn't think it was very much of an occupation.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how, wasn't there a lot of opposition in the part, especially of women against, you know, against the whole drinking?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes, yes. They were very opposed. There was a very active WCTU.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And the churches a lot were very, very against it. I think the Methodist Church is very strict on that. I think Episcopal is not as much. The Catholics not as much, but I think most of the other churches were very strict. Well, I've wondered about the opposition, and I have had... The Presbyterian's not quite as much, but uh... Methodist and the Baptist and uh... Some of the others were quite strict on that, I think. And the WCTU was quite... Well, not just a bunch of old women talking in those days, it was the younger women.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Um... I always told the story about one woman who I know at times drank something which you'd be at the fine parties, they did serve wine. Um...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the better parties?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, a lot of the better parties. I mean the bombers. And some of those, they did. They served wine. But she would be in that class too, but she belonged to WCTU, I think. They'd try and paint it, and they painted something that was for... The right type of thing to serve some wine, and they served some wine.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They said, she sat there and said, What am I drinking? What am I drinking?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But she drank it! And of course, it wasn't very much. That same lady also smoked, but it was without a lady in it. No, she smoked. And some of her age, she just concealed it. And I know one... My chum, her mother said, Please, to her, she smoked. And she said, Please don't let my friends see you smoking. You know, she had her club or her group there. It would be rather bad. She didn't want them to know that her daughter smoked. And, uh... It was kind of a big gesture on my mother's part one time when the same girl was at dinner at my place. And my mother said, Well, now we don't have any cigarettes here. When you have some, go ahead and smoke. Because Mother usually didn't allow it now. But she knew this girl liked a cigarette after a meal. And she did consent to her son. That sounds very open -minded. Well, she was rather. But she wouldn't have been as open -minded if I'd been doing it. Well, that's her daughter. No, she would have said, You shouldn't do that.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Very definitely, don't you know what that will do to you?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Probably, I would have been told.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was smoking supposed to lead to worse things?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, I think she said it just didn't sound good. It wasn't so good. No, no, she didn't think it would.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like drinking?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, no. And, in fact, my mother thought that if you could hold it, it was alright to have wine. It was only if you felt that you had to have it. Then you didn't. You stopped.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But, uh... This is it that I wondered about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much truth was there?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't want to just dwell on unpleasant subjects, but the idea of drinking, becoming alcoholic, and the dissolution of families, let's say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a real serious problem?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It happens sometimes. It still does. But I'm thinking of in those pioneering days. There were those. There were times when it did.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: There were times when it did. Not every time. But there were times, yes. I think an alibi was married to a man who drank entirely too much. It was kind of ploughed over in the home. But I think he did, and I think her life was really sad. He didn't provide an airplane for anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that much of the WCTU's concern was really about the dissolution of the family?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Probably. Probably.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And I think it was quite a religious thing. I think they were all rather devout church women that just found it was morally very bad. And some of them, of course, had husbands at that. They felt like they'd like to be a caring nation and get rid of it. No, I think it was one of those. I think it was very bound up in social strata, too. I think that they were... It was one little social clique. There was a lot in the social position.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was considered a high social...?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, they felt it was quite high.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I think. And proper. Very much like a lot of church people, too. I think it was kind of that way.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: A very civic kind of... Yes, yes. It could have been the society. Oh, yes.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And Bert Popper. I think that was the feeling. And then, of course, the younger bunch thought they were a bunch of old folks. You know. And they weren't going to do it that way. They were rather interested in dancing with the Charlestons and doing a lot of other things. But, no. The WCTU became the older women then. But they hadn't been, undoubtedly.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They'd been the younger ones at one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many of them women who had been concerned with women's rights prior to that? Was there much of a connection between the two?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Not necessarily. But I think out here, of course, women didn't have much struggle for rights.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You know. It was pretty well given to women. Women voted way back. You know, they always voted here. I had no constitution and all. It was very generous to women. You know, I mean, we didn't have that struggle. That happened in other places. But now here, it wasn't part of our life. Women had rights here. More. Women had always been more or less in business. Oh, I knew, um... Oh, fuck that. Self isn't tasked with them.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And they...

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He was a farmer. They ran a saloon in the early times in Lewiston. And he farmed.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He couldn't be trusted around the curve. He drank. He took a drink. He went off. And they were a very prominent early time in Lewiston. And, uh, he generally stayed in town and ran the saloon. And he ran the ranch. And when I was six... Oh, I was four years old. President Taft came to Lewiston. And my mother said, I'm going to take Taft's room because she may never see another living president. So, I think my father was renting a camera right there in this place. So my mother... took me across the bridge. And he was to come in the parade and go up to Fifth Street and talk. And have a shell bandstand. But she said, it wasn't necessary to do that, but she wanted me to at least see a living president. So, we got over there and somebody called. And it was, she said, uh, we were, you know, like a parade back the ways. And she said, I'll hold you when it comes by so you can see it. But I was too heavy to hold very long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Somebody called and said, Laura, that's your mother's name?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: That was my mother's name.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And so, they were in a boxcar. And we went over and they helped us. We got up in the boxcar and we sat in the boxcar. At least I didn't think it would be. And stood up, I guess. And President Taft came by. And we could see him very good. And he made a very special bow with his silk hat. There's people staying there, you know, to see him up in this boxcar. And the crowd in front. And that was, I can't say the name now, but it was very prominent in early days of the Westin. They were the people that, uh... She ran the saloon and he ran the ranch. Because, well, sometimes he was in the saloon, you know. But, um, he told my mother, he said, he got very drunk once and she was trying to tow him up. He came back on the ranch. She shut him up down the basement. And it was where the barrels of liquor were. But he didn't think he could get at them because he had no way of getting to it. Nothing to drink on him. But he had a broomstick. And he'd get fat in and he'd get a drop or two. And he managed to keep himself drunk for quite a while. And finally, Annie found out how he was doing it. And she took the broomstick away from him. And she got him sober and sent him back to the ranch. She was a tall, thin woman. But she was not, no, she was not a small. She may have been a little bit gay. I don't know, queer. But, um, she was playing business. She wore Mother Hubbard. It was not attractive. Her hair was done up here playing. And she was all business. And she was ready. But she ran the saloon and they got ahead. And they were respected, as I say, by the bankers and all that. And ranching had me a good ranch. And they had quit the saloon along at this time and they were both on the ranch. But, um, they were very typically colorful people of the 90s in Lewiston. And I can't say her name. Her name was Annie.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Very prominent in those times. And I was kind of pretty, I saw them once and knew who they were. Because they were tough, tough people. And they both knew my mother because she'd done the saloon. And they had business with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think it had?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, he was so pink and white and looked like, um, oh, a huge Santa Claus, you might say. And so pleasant and so smiling. And he had tails and silk hats and he was very elegant. He was plump and oh, so smiling. Oh, I was quite impressed. Didn't look like anybody else. He looked like that. He really did look like his pictures, but he was kind of pink and white too, you know. His skin and he was so smiling and his eyes were very bright. He was really a very pleasant person, evidently. I suppose he had serious ties, undoubtedly a lot. But he was just all bows and smiles and the silk hat. Boy, the silk hat, you know. I'd only seen the men in the comic strips, I think. Nobody else I knew wore a silk hat. My father wore a black Stetson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Always a black Stetson until way late in the years when they finally said, for goodness sakes, why wear that hat?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And I know Mr. Dustin said, you never know. His new hat is identical to his old hat. He's six -year -old. For many years, that was all he would ever wear. But Mr. Taff wore a silk hat. And he's...oh, cut away. Cut away. Oh, yes. He's very elegant. The whole bit. This thing's elegant. Very elegant. Later, the politicians, generally you could tell, my mother always called them Mr. Broadcloths. But they wore very well tailored dark suits and their shoes were beautifully polished. That was the way Mr. Oh,

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: the Congressman, Bertonell French, always loved them. Just in a very well -fitting suit and very well -polished shoes. But now it's like when he came to town because he talked to my mother and always came to see us. And I always thought it was nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the ... from Palouse?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes. They went to school together. They went to school. The French people, the French family had a farm out from there. And their father gave a lecture in town. Charged admission. And it was 15 or 25 cents or something. And everybody wondered what he would have to say. He was just a farmer and the children went to school and they wore little rather old -fashioned clothes. Big shoes. Or dressed slightly different from some of the others. A little more old -fashioned, panel -room type of thing.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And he went to home and he gave this lecture. And my mother said the two little boys sat up there. Kind of stiffly in front of me. Kind of embarrassed. And he sat up in the center of the stage and he had a knife and he was wearing this piece of wood. And he said everybody was there and they paid to get in. For a while he said, You'll always get it from you. You'll never cut yourself. He put a little wood. Ten more minutes he made that remark again. Yeah, that was all he said. And after a while they'd leave and they'd see some bus in the street and they'd say, You heard Mr.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: French's lecture?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well you ought to hear it. And so I guess he got everybody's, whatever it was, the boys sat up there rather stiffly, kind of embarrassed. And one of them became a congressman and one of them became a college president. But they had a sister too. But Burton and my mother always called by the making. And they talked all the time and had fun together. And he'd always be very nice to Laura's little girl. Shake hands with me and that was something that shook hands with a congressman and not everybody in the school did that. So I thought that was quite evident. But my mother thought that was probably quite an embarrassing experience for the little boys. You know you talked about your mother's childhood is when she was rather sickly. She was very sickly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this unusual for kids?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, to a degree. And of course people were not very nice about it. They used to say, Too bad she'll never grow up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And after she had a growth on her neck, well how does it feel to know you're going to die?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You know, nothing about how you proper carefulness for sick people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the main sickness that she had?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, as a child she probably had a very bad case of adenine. And she had a guitar. Her ears bothered her from then. I think that was the main thing that was wrong with her. She survived all the children things. Her brother of course died of typhoid, I mean of diphtheria. And her mother died of typhoid. Which was something that shouldn't happen but it did in those times. And she was only 14 when her mother died. And they had big plans. It would have been nice. Her mother was the milliner in Palouse but she wasn't always going to stay in Palouse. She wanted to get to a larger city. And she would have. And my mother had quite a bit of a milliner who probably would have worked with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But as a milliner was she contributing substantially to the family?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I think so. I think so.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Not that my grandfather at that time wasn't able to support them. He did. But she was doing very well but she didn't have long enough time. And then it was kind of sold out and didn't bring too much. Because there was nobody to blame. My mother was too young. They wouldn't turn over to a 14 year old girl. So that was it. But um. And she had. After she had finally been to Chicago and had her surgery safely done. She had been sick for quite a while. She had gone to a boarding school in Spokane. So she had to go away to school. And her sister had gone to St. Helens in Portland. Which was a Catholic boarding school for everybody in the Northwest. But if they could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When if they could?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Catholic or not?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, Jewish. No matter. It was the nice boarding school. And they were taught to be young ladies. And they were taught how to do fine sewing. And how to paint a picture. They all painted the mouth good and oil as a rule. And a few other things. And um. More emphasis on that than on practical things. And how to learn a living. Because there weren't things for them to do. They were expected problems in memory. The Jewish girls in Lewiston went to St. Helens. I think some of them. If they had money. And I know one from Colfax did. And um. You didn't have to be a Catholic. But then it was school. But um. My mother went. Got no farther than the old Spokane College in Spokane. Which was I think a Methodist college. And they called it a college which was a prep school. Really. Although they did some of them go out there and preach from there. But it was more of a prep. They were taught algebra. Things that you taught in high school more or less. And um. There was a Miss Hope was the elocution teacher. And a professor Rippetole taught algebra. And Mother Sid was very good. Couldn't explain it beautifully. The food was rather bad like some of the Dickens stories. And um. They sat at different tables and she said Miss Hope would always start out dishing. She served rather generously and ended up very poorly. And served herself last with very poor stuff. And the food was not very good except on Thanksgiving. And it was a very fine feast in the Aachen. But she wondered if she thought it would be nice if it would spread out a little better. And there was a girl who went there named Kate Hoggett. Who became Catherine Ridgway. And gave readings and was on the stage. And toured the country and was well known. Pictures in Boston and what have you. And Miss Hope had said she was starved physically and spiritually and intellectually in her job. But she needed the job. She was the teacher. And um. She was uh. The elocution teacher. Promed on Sunday English. And she.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is the same girl that went on?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No one of her. One of her students. One of her students. Her best student. And um. She said that Miss Hope had a way of drawing out people at the table in conversation. There was one very sincere nice young man that was going to be a minister and didn't have much to say. But one of them would certainly make a fool of himself. Of what he wanted to be. He got all the chicken dinners and he had all the fun. That was his reason for wanting to be a minister. And he didn't realize how much he was giving himself away. But um. Um. After that she went to school some in Palouse. So you see it wasn't that advanced to school. When the teacher would come and um. Well it was the um. Then Dicton nuns came up and taught school. From Colton. The ones that were later at Cottonwood. And um. They taught at school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At Palouse?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Uh huh. And um. My mother went to that. There were two sisters that taught. They taught the upper and the lower grades. And Sister and Nate Kale taught the upper grade. And then there was one that kept counts for it. There were three sisters there. And there was no Catholic church in Palouse. But once in a while a minister, I guess a priest came through. There was an Episcopal church. And a Christian church. The elder right lived there. And the Christian was a very strong church in this area. And the Methodist. And I don't know some others probably. But um. But your mother went to school with this. To this school. To this school. Uh huh. And they all loved the sister Nate Kale. And they were Swiss. I believe. And they had come over to this country. And she was very prominent in the hospital when they were at Cottonwood. And people knew her. Uh. Knew her. She was a very prominent sister. And very very respected in all the community. But she was the teacher there. And Mother said she pronounced a few things in a foreign way. But she said you distribute the papers. Sister distribute them. It was very formal. But she got quite a little bit from that school to learn some more. And then when she. The thing was you could take a test and you could teach school. And as I say it wasn't a very good job. Two months and like that. Or you could be a housemaid. You could be a dressmaker. Or a milliner. And um. They were beginning once in a while to clerk but not much. My grandfather owned a little stock in the store up there. But um. There wasn't much opening for a girl. And there was a big telephone that comes in town. And the girl that had been in the telephone office. Was there for four months. And she was leaving. And so my father knew her folks. So he went and talked to her. And he said how about Laura for the job. My grandfather. And so she said where for Laura to come. And so she learned that we a telephone operator. Which was one of the few jobs open. And you made as much as a school teacher. Every month and you worked twelve months a year. Of course you worked twelve hours a day. And you worked six and a half days a week. A little more than a school teacher would. But then you lived in one place. So after she had been there a while. They ordered a game that they needed for Lewiston. And they were going to take the one that she had trained to help her. That was training one for a spare or whatever. And she decided that she was going to go. And they said well yes if you want to. She decided she wanted to come to Lewiston. So she came to Lewiston. My mother always wondered when she was in Chicago. And did finally have successful surgery on her neck after years of the wrong thing. It was a Dr. Frank. A Jewish doctor in this Catholic hospital. He was the head surgeon at St. Elizabeth's that did the surgery. And you know on that very famous murder case of Leopold and Leopold. They killed a Jewish boy who was from a wealthy Jewish family. His name was Frank. And my mother always wondered if it wasn't from that same head. And they were I guess as I understand very bitter about the whole thing. They never wanted the people released. And I can understand how they feel. Because they're from the nice boy who had been murdered brutally. But she had very fine feelings for this Dr. Frank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did she finally go to Chicago?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well it was after her mother had died. Her neck was worse again. And it was a separating tumor which nowadays a good doctor could probably do in his office. But they didn't know what to do. Surgery was very new. And they tried. And of course they drained it. They tried. They cut it open. They decided finally she swallowed her tongue but they didn't know that. So they rolled her in a barrel and saved her life that way. And they thought she couldn't take either. They tried it with alcohol once. It's dead in the pain so they could cut it open because it would swell up. Have to be drained. Lost the nerve. Had to get another doctor to sort of. And she said it just made her sick. It didn't end in the pain. And they didn't know what to do. And sometimes it would heal up for a time and it would break out again. Everybody thought it must be cancer and she would die. And there was sometimes had something done wall to wall and she was getting ready thinking about going there. And then that person died and she said he probably did have cancer. And they read in the paper about a very prominent doctor from Chicago. Came west at $1 ,000 a day to treat somebody in San Francisco. Well they couldn't pay a doctor $1 ,000 a day to come from Chicago to treat her but they could send her to Chicago. So my grandfather sent her to Chicago. And she said a very green girl from Palouse City, Washington territory went alone to Chicago. And she got a very decent place to stay. This is Ireland I think her name was. And she went to this doctor's office and he was an internist. He wasn't a surgeon at all. They didn't know the difference and he wasn't good at this. She said he dressed at the leg of the hamster just like they had done here and the nurse dressed it. She wasn't getting anyplace at all and she was very very discouraged. So money didn't come from home. The mail didn't get through right. So she was running out of money and she didn't eat right. And with all this strain on her system she needed a lot of food. And she probably caught a cold and then she collapsed. Well she fainted. She was downtown or something. So they took her to an ambulance to St. Elizabeth's Hospital which was in the suburbs at that time of Chicago. And not a big hospital at all. And it was a huge place in the high cast. And it was a Catholic hospital with my sisters. And she always laughed and she kind of came to and was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One sister came in and she wanted to know, find out about her and she says, Are you Catholic or Protestant?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And Mother didn't watch it at all. She said, I didn't know them just after Protestant. I forgot how she answered it. It was very different. They always laughed about it afterwards. But anyway they took care of her and they saw this neck and they called in their head surgeon. It was Dr. Frank. He said, we'll build you up and take care of that. And it didn't cost much to stay in the hospital in those days. So what her father was spending here was ample. You could live there very cheap in the hospital. And they kept borders too. I mean some people they had one section where they had some old women that were a little bit batty and they kept them there. And there were interns there and people that were there quite a while. So she stayed in St. Elizabeth's a long time. She was a doctor who had been a doctor Frank operating on her successfully. And then she stayed in Chicago a while because there was an outpatient. She took a course in dress making that she apprenticed to a fashionable dress maker while she was there. She read and read and saw about it. So she learned quite a bit about that and then came home to Palouse. And then later, as I say, went to work in the telephone office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Dariana tell you about what it was like to her to be in a huge city like that?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh yes, quite a bit. She learned Chicago. Mother always learned any city or any place she was. Like on her vacation trip to San Francisco she saw everything from Chinatown to Nob Hill. She was thorough in it. And she saw quite a bit of Chicago. She knew all the parks and all the big stores and all the things to go to. Oh yes. And Mrs. Ireland, where she stayed, had lived there even through the fire. And had told her they used to go at night and evening and watch it where it would be burning in some parts. It would burn in different parts and it lasted a long time. And she went to a few good theater things when she was there all she could.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: So she found it exciting rather than scary, frightening. Well, she said, yes, rather.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Of course it was not the big city it was later when I was through there. But still it was a big city. But she was rather fortunate in where she stayed, this place. And this woman's son had a grocery route. I think it was just something. People had routes. In those days you didn't always go to a store. They came and took your orders and delivered. And he had that. And he knew a lot about Chicago too. And she learned from them and different people. Had lots of close friends at the hospital and the interns and different ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she know something of a personal friendship with the doctor?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, well they all just almost worshiped him. They thought he was so nice and he was so good to all of them. She said there was one girl there that was very impoverished. She had been sent from Texas to go to a Catholic school. And her mother was very anti -Catholic but she always had something there. And she had a brother who had gone. And there were three children anyway in the family. And he had become a Catholic and was disowned. And he had written his sister and told her to study it. She did. She became a Catholic. And her mother was disowned. And so then she wanted to be a nun and thought she did at the time. But she was sick and she was at this hospital. And she did beautiful paintings. And she said Dr. Frank was always buying some of her paintings out of the way. And things that way. And she'd give one, oh don't do that, I'll buy it from you. She said he was so nice to everybody. And she said he was tall, more of a Van Dyke beard. Very dignified I think. But she said if Jesus had been a brunette, he looked like he would have imagined. He would look very much like probably the real Jesus looked. Because I don't think since he was unduly Jewish I know that he was a blonde. But anyway she said he looked except for that. And they all liked him very much. And he was the head surgeon. They had other doctors in this. And of course there were some interns and they leaned there while they played tennis with the interns. And they really had quite a topic. And there were girls there for different reasons. And one of them I guess was there because she was inclined to drink. And Mother went with her shopping sometimes. And in some way she pulled a faint and got a drink. And Mother had an awful time getting her back and they were pretty angry at her. And then she wrote to her family and she wanted some money. They wouldn't let her have any money. And she wanted certain things so she got her brother to send it to Mother. And she bought her this fancy work and all that. But finally, the second largest hotel in Chicago at the time, I don't know the name of it. It wasn't the Palmer House. It was another one. Wife, evidently went on a run. And she arrived to be sober up with her maid. And they put her in a certain room and they had had her before. And some way or other this Amanda and she were kindred spirits about drink. And so she got some money, some of this money. And anyway they got some drink. And Mother had a very angry sister facing her about that money. But she wasn't supposed to do that. Well she counted for every bit of it but then they were back and they had quite a time. And there was a famous surgery case of pre -pending and brain surgery. And she was in on that. Went with the girls some places. Went around Chicago with different ones, different things, seeing different things. Went with this one that thought she wanted to be a sister one time to another place. And she said to her, these women were so old -passionately dressed. Everything was out of style and dowdy. Dull and dowdy. And she said this girl was calling her sister. And she said that. She said, oh yes, they belong to an order from England. Where they were prosecuted, they were Catholics. They were sisters, they were well -being hung. So they didn't wear the habits, you know, the same. And they were, she said, well they were the most kind of trains and old -fashioned things. But also dowdy looking and she said it seems so strange to have them called sisters. But Saint Elizabeth was not the big husband that they became. They had insane people there. One woman that always thought she would starve to death. And so all her food, she was always hiding it. And she would stuff the hem of her dresses and everything. And every so often one sister would go in. You didn't see her hair, but Mother said that Dowdy had red hair and she'd go and clean her up. And one of them that always put her furniture up on top of each other. She stacked it and stood on top of it. She would be on the chair on top of a dresser and the stool on top of the chair. And one that always came out and said, all is tote. All is dead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All? What?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: All is tote. Everything was dead with her. That was what it meant to her. What is tote means. Evidently. And they had somebody stay with her. And the family was evidently wealthy. And they had a girl who spoke her language stay with her. But once in a while she'd be out of the way and she'd get away and she'd run into the other rooms and grab them and say, oh, there's tote. Unless it's scary to death. Almost if you were coming out of the ether or something. And they found out that she could take either, but that she swallowed her tongue. And I guess they swallowed her tongue. Always swallowed her tongue and ran and said it again. She did always swallow her tongue.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who? You?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: My mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mama did it all?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: She'd swallow her tongue. And she told, she had other surgeries. She told the doctor, I mean, there was a time I swallowed my tongue. It's a thing. You watch it. And certain people do. I have a friend who always swallowed her tongue. And they have very sore tongues afterwards. It's interesting. The Chicago Hospital, they had, they institutionalized people in the hospital. Yeah. They wouldn't know, but it wasn't that busy. Well, I know, I hear it was Orofino and... Oh, well, these were wealthier people and their family. It was like a private sanitarium part, you see. And there were several wealthy people there. Or the women. And most of them spoke another language. A lot of them. But, um, and, as I say, people from business that they would sober up once in a while. Well, they do that once in a while in the hospital now, I guess. I've known of that happening in St. Joseph's in the old days. I don't know what to do, they knew now. But, of course, I was quite familiar with St. Joseph's as a child. Climbed in back of the pendulum in that big clock once. And I was quite small. Tried to get in. I don't think I got very far, but I tried. And, um, my father was out there down there. When I was four years old for appendicitis and he had gangrene. He came through fine. Which was quite a deal in those days in 1910. And he was in there a long time. He was hospitalized a long time. And any time my mother wanted to go to the hospital, she took me and I and I went to the sister's house. They took care of me. Always. That was one reason when she had an eye infection, she debated about going to Spokane or going to Lewiston. And if she'd gone to Spokane, she would have gone to Dr. Hopkins who was married to a polymer. I think he was a very good doctor. I think she would have been better off with him. But she decided it would be so easy to come to Lewiston. My father was very busy. It was a busy time in the cannery and he wouldn't have much time to look out for me. And, um, in Lewiston, she'd just bring me along and I'd go to the sister's house. They took out for me. Now, of course, it's not a bit like that. It's very institutionalized, but in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she was your mother's Catholic?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: My father was. And they were married in the church. One time my mother, and they always did a lot of things for the sisters. We had an acreage in the fruit. Vegetables was always given to the hospital. Most of that was from our place for years. And they never accepted pain. So the sisters were very, felt very loyal to us. I can remember saying on their lap as a child, you know, mother board you and come hold me. And I had no feeling at all. But I know that, um, when I was six years old, my mother was very sick and dad was out of town. And we were on, we weren't living in Clarkson. I assume we own these places over here, but they had had the cannery, the company, had had a cannery on Snake River Avenue. And we had stayed across the street at Edna Pierce's. Edna Miller. Miller Grades, named for her. Edna was old -time Lewis. Snake River was beginning to be very down men, commercial and not nice, but Edna still lived there. And, um, well she was, as I say, contemporary, the Baltimore girls and all that, and accepted by everybody. And it was the perfect thing, my mother went over and we stayed dinner. And, uh, it was at her house and mother was very sick. Oh, she was just having a terrible time. She couldn't breathe. So, she almost choked to death one night. And so she decided that she needed nursing. So she went up to the hospital, she said, I'm going to be put to bed and taken care of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who's your doctor?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't want a doctor, I just need nursing. Mrs. Mann, we will lose our license if we do that.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Finally they couldn't turn Mrs. Mann down, so they kept Mrs. Mann. And they had a sign on the door and I went, as usual, over to the sisters' house. And, um, every sister came in and did something for her. She had her throat sprayed and she had a goggle and she had this and she had that. And I think they all did everything they could think of to do, because they mustn't let her die, because they would lose their right to practice if they did that. Right to practice. Well, I mean, to run the hospital. They would lose their license, oh my. She was in there without a doctor, they would be in real trouble. So they all worked on her very hard and they liked her. So, in a few days she was better. We came home and she started to whoop. She had whooping cough at about 40. And she had been very sick, of course, with whooping cough. So, I had whooping cough too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what she had had with whooping cough?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, with whooping cough. But she hadn't whooped yet, you see. She didn't do it until after she got home. They didn't know, they thought she had a coop or a back comb or something, you know, wrong with her. She could, but it loosened up, you see, with all this attention. Oh, no space, no space. You name it, she had it for a while, I know. And she said, all I need is good nursing. And she got it.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, you said before that she had a great attraction to all sorts of cures, remedies, and that thing. Oh, yes.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: She went to the osteopaths and she... There weren't chiropractors in those days, but all those rub doctors, I was drug to every one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is that what a chiropractor was, an osteopath in those days?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, they weren't. We didn't have chiropractors. That came later. They were osteopaths and different kinds of homeopathic, you name it. But osteopath was the best trained of any of them, but there were others. But they had great faith in a lot of them, along with being very, very strict on good doctrine, because she'd had this background at St. Elizabeth's, the best of surgery, and the best of that, and complete faith in it, and then she'd fall for all these other things, too. And every fad that came along in diet or anything else. And always good fast and starving. That caused a lot of trouble by getting too much strong willpower about that. It was quite a fad to fast, and mother could always fast longer than anybody else.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And wasn't much you can do about she. He was anything like that. She went the whole bit. And, oh, yes, she was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it because of her bad health when she was growing up?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't know. She just got interested and she was a little bit gullible.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Quite a little bit gullible on that, I think. Well, I know. All those old patent remedies and everything... Oh, she didn't take much of that. No. But that had been so popular that I have a feeling it was a frugal field, too. Well, you see, people died for so many things. No, she didn't like the patent medicine. She knew a girl who blamed... ...pill for pale people and her mother had taken them and died. Because she didn't get proper medical attention, you know. The pills didn't kill her, but it was the medical attention that she didn't get. That was the main thing. The trouble was... Oh, but the pen comes. Medicine has some value for certain things, but it didn't do half what they claimed it did. I mean, you name it, it cured baldness and kidney trouble and fallen arches and the whole bitch, you know. It's out your spine. It took care of your stomach. And no, medicine did all that, you know. And Mother was quite advanced on that in the early times in Lewiston. She stayed at Piersdorf's after she left the Thatcher home. When she came to Lewiston, she went to the Raymond house. And that was 50 cents a meal. She couldn't stay there very long. So she stayed at Thatcher's and then at Piersdorf's. And Mrs. Piersdorf's had a daughter who was deaf. And probably deaf from diphtheria or something, you know. But had not had good attention and it affected her ears. You know, infection. They didn't know what to do in those days since she'd gone deaf. Laura. Her name was Laura like my mother's. And Laura had an infection in her hand. And their family doctor was a very good doctor, but not modern at all. And he didn't know what to do for infections. Now he knew measles when he saw them and scarlet fever and smallpox and chickenpox and those things. He was pretty good on them, but he didn't know. Probably was pretty good on diphtheria and typhoid, you know, as good as they were. But he didn't know what to do on infections. And he was posting red -note posts. Mother knew it was wrong because that wasn't the way they did it at St. Elizabeth's. They didn't do things like that at all. It was very different. She knew that. And the hand was getting worse and the infection was going in. So she, Dr. Shaff was a more modern doctor and was her doctor. And so she just knew that that child should go to a better doctor. She'd lose her hand, she'd lose her arm, might lose her life. She got very worked up. So she talked to Grandma Pierce about it. Well, Grandma didn't like to change doctors because he'd done such a good doctorate to her family. And she said, well, if she can't, this is just too much. Well, all right, Laura, you feel that way, you can take her. She wanted to take her, so I'll take her. So then she had to argue with Shaff. He was Morris's patient, he wouldn't touch her. Well, I have permission to take her to you. So that's all right. Well, no, she said, look, doctor. He's holdusing it with bread and milk.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: What?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: She prescribed, well, that wasn't right. She said, now look here, that girl's already deaf.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You want her to be one -armed?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, bring her in. And of course, I don't know why he did something very different and he cured it. And she said, if it had been the other way around, Dr. Morris didn't speak to her for about six months. But if it had been Dr. Shaff in the other decision, he never would have spoke to her again. But Morris couldn't say mad woman then. He was a different turn, you know. But the two doctors in yellow made it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, by the way, how Laura got along? Who was deaf?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes, she got along fine. She talked this way and read lips beautifully. And her children, she has grandchildren who are doctors now. It's okay, very fine. Some of the best. She married and her children talked to her this way, you know, with a hand. My mother did. They could talk one hand or both. I don't know, I couldn't do it. I know I and oh, a few things, but I couldn't begin to do teeth. But mother always could talk to her and they always were friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She couldn't make herself understood to your mother?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes. Well, she talked, but she couldn't hear. She lost her hearing after she already knew how to talk. Oh, yes, yes, after. And it was some childhood sickness or something, probably something with a throat, you know, and your ears are so easily affected. She went deaf. And she wasn't born yet. And she raised a family, a very nice family. She did very well. And her children were very fine. Her mother is Irene Bozarth, called the second. She's lovely. And her children went to Harvard Medical School. Well, they did all right. But it would have been too bad if she lost her arm. You know, it would have been another handicap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of those nature paths and on -seat paths?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, I didn't like them much. And now my mother was very strict on some of them. There were some that were, oh, not really what you call the rub doctors, but the other kind, you know, the therapies that, oh, they got their diplomas in the mail by paying so much money, like this business and the ministers who had just been hearing about their thing for $50. And mother was quite horrified at a lot of that. And of course, I always was. And she said she kind of got in trouble with one of them at the time. She, one of them, she thought very unscrupulous, Lewis. And she, a very attractive young woman was there with her son. And he was not doing well at all. And they ate at the same hotel a few times, because mother often ate out working. And that was down in the France.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she said, well, why don't you take him to a good doctor?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And she had a good doctor. And of course, very indignant. Most anything more, and the boy died, but she married the doctor. And mother felt he was just completely beyond the pale. And I know Dr. Shaff and some of them thought he ought to have been run out of town, you know. And he wasn't recognized in the medical profession, but they had a lot of that around. Now, I don't know, Moxley may have had training as a doctor, and he was the drugist. And he, the Moxley Building is over there, and the Moxley Sisters, yes, sister's still here. And I read something about, he had more training, and he was a drugist too. But I know, and this was one unusual thing, my mother got a felon. And you know what that is, it's a fan infection on the finger. And I was just horrified. I was only six years old, about scared, people got blood -piercing, and people died. My mother must not die, mothers, you know. And I was just very upset, and I thought she should go to the best doctor, who usually went to Shaff. Who gave you the most bitter medicine ever, and invented them. The worse it tasted, the better it was for you, must be his theory. Otherwise, he was nice. I liked him. But, no, she said, I'm going to get Moxley. I was calling him Doc Moxley. And it was rather an old -fashioned drug story, by that time. It was down, you know, it's the Moxley Building is there. And there was more herbs and things around, I think a little bit, than some of them. And I went with her, and I was quite fearful. And he gave her some pink pills. And she got better at being with her. Everybody, they had anything wrong with their fingers, and Winston went to doctors. And I know I've talked about some of the other second -generation people from these old -timers. And she always went there for fingers. And he had something, he gave people for infection and fingers. And he was a drugist. Well, he may have had some, he was more of a doctor, certainly, than these others were. But he practiced more as a drugist. But they called him Doc, and he may have had some doctor training. I think he did. I think what his sister said, he mustn't have training in that line. But he ran a drug store. He ran a drug store, that was his plan. He practiced medicine on the side. Yes, a little bit. But his main thing had been that, whatever he could give, and other people told him, he had something that, I don't know, he had penicillin without knowing whether or not, or something that he gave, he knew some drug for that, that was just evidently. And I talked to her, I don't know how many, oh yes, she always went to him for fingers. No matter what else she did. So your mother knew his reputation for fingers. She knew his friend, and I would go to Doc Markson. I wonder if he gave everyone the same thing. I don't know. Something for infection, evidently.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother ever have any dealings with Foster?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, that was how the candy got in to him. She heard about Dr. Foster. And so, of course, she decided I was going to Dr. Foster. I wasn't quite that well. Actually, I wasn't really very sick either, but you. Yes, so she took me up to Dr. Foster. And I think I went mostly to Dr. Marsh, who was a, um, a Gnostic path graduate. And when we were in Juliaetta, she decided that that was a very good place for a candy. There was a lot of fruit. And she came back and that was my father. The Camrys were victims in those days. And so, they talked about it. And he was with the TreadGrows Association, and they decided to build a canyony at junior. Because my mother had taken me up to Dr. Foster. And so, Dr. Foster left, you know, came down here. But they built a canyony. And that gave, you know, after they lost Foster, because when Foster was there, you know, everybody could want to keep a boarder, kept a boarder. Or run the room. And gave a little money in the town, as well as the hotels. And then they were just going to starve to death. There was no business, you know. And they were so mad at Foster for leaving. Oh dear. But the camera came. And then they could all work in the camera. They could all make a little money. Juden's always been a little lucky.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Foster like?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, of course I think he used a kind of acid to remove skin cancer. Now I doubt that he did anything beyond that. But it did work on skin cancer in the early stages. Like, they use a light needle and that now. He had what would be considered, what my mother would call the power of suggestion. He wasn't quite a faith healer, and yet almost. No. And he did have a certain something. A magnetism. And he could read your mind, apparently. Because I got very panicky down here when I realized I might have a boarder. And to me, a boarder was very bad. You had a boarder, you were operating on, you died. Because one girl, the only one we knew, had had a boarder operation, she died. And that, to me, even to this day, I would be a very bad boarder operation patient. Because I would still remember this girl that died. And it would be very... I know I said to a friend of my father, I'd think of her, and she died. I couldn't help it. She said, I know better, but I still do it. We know that that is necessary, but it still would be a little much. And so at 15, I'm without saying that I had a boarder. And it's not very noticeable. I don't suppose you'd notice I had a boarder. But it isn't exterior. Well, I was quite upset. So my mother took me to Boston. And he looked at me and he says, you're pretty healthy outside that boarder. I said, okay. One other doctor, he says, I said, but you're a boarder. And only two doctors, the other was a... And he gave me something to put on, a brilliant thing for it. It was down in Largent Sand, I think. But anyway, I was in bed clothes for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went to see Foster, what was it for?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: For the boarder. Just for that. Before I ever told him. He said, you're pretty well except for the boarder. This power of suggestion... Well, he had a certain way. Now, he wouldn't call himself a faith healer, but he would say to you, you're going to be dead on something and you, Jeremy, are. Now, I don't know. It's something there, you know. And of course, he had a daughter my age, I went to school with. And she was very much like her father, Levo. The other children weren't. They were all their mother's children. But Levo was her father. So very, very close to him. And he always blamed himself for her death. And he was drinking. And he didn't drink. That was his besetting sin. And he didn't. He said he didn't take care of her. And she died. She got pneumonia and died. Whether or not, you know, I think she probably had care. But maybe he could have done something.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Who knows?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Anyway, from that time on, Dr. Foster was... He gave up, you might say. He drank too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean after she died?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: After he died, he was never the same fellow. He never got over grieving for Levo. She was my age. We were in the same class in high school. Been in the grade schools together. And she was more his turn. Now, I think he was a horrible man for his wife to live with. But... And she was a nice person. But, um... And he had two boys that I knew in school. Bob was a little older. And Bob became some kind of doctor. He was important. And he tried to work with his father some. And they studied and went off and studied and became some type of chiropractic or something. And not that they had a license to practice. And, uh, Babe lives in Lewiston now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Babe?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Uh huh. The younger girl. Her husband.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His daughter?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Uh huh. Her husband is one of the partners in the Greyhound Bus Depot. Mr. Nelson and whatever his name is. And his wife is Babe. And we talked about it not long ago. She was in, helping out in the restaurant there. And she doesn't work there very much. But she did a little bit. And I said, you know, I'll always remember something your father told me. He said, I didn't say a fella, but I really might do so. Don't you ever say that. Don't you ever say it and you don't know the pressures. You knew probably just what they knew. Don't say that. And she left. She said, yes, he said that to me so many times. Don't you ever say that. If I were them, I'd do so and so. Because you don't know what you'd do. You'd make the same mistakes they make. And, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You figure he'd been hard to live with because of his drinking mainly? Or because of the kind of person he was?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: His drinking man, he was difficult, I imagine. I think that she was more formal. She had come to him, I understand, for something he'd cured her. And that was how they met. But, of course, when he was drinking, he was hopeless at times. She might have company and he might come in and proceed to eat peas with his knife. And he'd say, now I know my wife doesn't like news. She's going to kick me on the table, but this is what I like to do. I'm your first woman without murdering, you know. Or something else. And the boys were always meeting. Older boys would get in more trouble. But they weren't bad kids and they turned out all right. Only Charlie died. But, um. Bob used to run away from home. And finally Charlie would run away from home and he'd come back after so long. And they said Charlie ran away and Bob went in the kitchen and looked around. He said he'll be back in so many days. Two days or one. He knew he'd taken just that much food. He'd done that so many times himself. He used to do it that way. But they weren't bad, you know. But they were kind of prominent kids in Juliaetta, you know. He was the big man. He was never that much down here. Probably was a mistake to move here. In a way. He was THE man in Juliaetta. And he wasn't, you know. The other doctors were, hmm. Oh no. He was a no -no. And yet some of them sort of respected him too. And he did have a certain personal magnetism that was really something. He was a smart man and very peculiar. Very southern. Not exactly educated and certainly not dumb.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he say he was southern?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't know.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And he was not a normal doctor. No. He was an osteopath. Well, no. He may have had some degree but I don't again very much. But he was more of a nature path. Yes. Yes. He had one osteopath working for him. But no. He'd never gone to school that much. Well, you know, in the old... But he could tell you something to do on your diet that was good for you. He'd look at you and say, you should eat this or you should eat that. He was very good on that. He could give you a lot of help on diets. Very practical help. Different things he told me on diets that were really good. You know. And he knew. And different things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he could tell by looking at you?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Just looking at you. And I think he read your mind. I think he was a really kind of a... I think he had him sort of collectively. I think he did that. I think he was ESP very much. Without consciously maybe knowing it. And I think he sort of knew he did. He had that ability some way. And he transferred it to you some way. An impression in some way. He had a... There was a certain magnetism about the man.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He, I heard, had lectured too. He used to give lectures in Juliaetta. I guess he did sometimes. I was pretty small. And I of course didn't know much about that. But he might have, yes.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And... No, they had a nice home. He was the prominent man. And he never was quite as... You know, there was more opposition down here. There was the regular MDs that frowned very much, you see. And there was no MD up there. He was ill. There was one drugstore. And the first drugstore, Marsh, had been the druggist. And he got interested in fostering. He became an osteopath and looked for them. Then there was another druggist, Dr. Row, later. But they... He was the big man. And he wasn't so much here, you see. He wasn't so unique. He was important, but not that much. But people did come from all over. And I think he did cure skin cancer. Not internally.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And there's quite a bit of skin cancer, right?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And they didn't know what to do for it.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And his acid did take it off. And it was acid. He burned it up. Yeah, I've heard a number of people remember people walking up and down the street in Juliaetta painting with that, you know, those poults. Oh yes, yes. They had those all over. They had them down here.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And it was acid and it burned it off. Now, you go to a dermatologist now and he does it a little smoother, probably. But the same thing, I think, more or less. It was an acid. He burned it off. And just so you got it all along, like, words. Do you know why he came...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: why he left?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I heard he got in a fight with Porter up there. Well, he might have. He might have. I don't know too much about him. Porter was always easy to get along with either. L .A.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You mean the banker?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yeah, the banker. Yeah, he might have. Porter was always easy to get along with. Of course, Mr. Dustin didn't like Porter at all. The bank... The bank down here. And Mr. Dustin's credit, he was financing it. He had the money more than my father. And we had a bookkeeper. First, it was real good. Then she had promised to stay the whole summer. But she had a good job offered her that was permanent in the courthouse. And she stayed there the rest of her life. And so she told my father about it. She said, but I did promise to stay, so I will. But she said, Ian Lesh, you are willing for me to go. He said, no, I won't stand in your way from that kind of a job. It was just an ordinary job, yes. But for that, no. And she said, well, I know, so and so has had the same training and she could do it. Well, they got this May person. May didn't keep the books right.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And my father said, now is there enough money in the bank without transferring any more funds for all these checks I'm going to be giving?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes, if I need money. And there was tons and tons of cherries to be paid for. And all the payroll. So, he signed the checks, she made them out. And they were $10 ,000 for overdrawing. And that's a deal of money, you know. And they had, that is the, Juliaetta, they hadn't put it from Clarkson up there. And so they were turning them down. And that was terrible for the company. When you're new in town, if you're not, your checks are going to bounce. Oh, Mr. Justin was so mad. They should have honored them and called them. Well, they would have here, but see, they didn't know about them. My father said, you couldn't blame Mr. Porter. And my folks always kept their small checking account there. But Mr. Dustin wouldn't have no more account in Juliaetta. The banking was always on the clock, so bank from then on. And, but, um, Porter, of course, always hired Mrs. Green to teach school. He was the kind of big man in the town. And Mrs. Green was not a good teacher. Her daughters were. But she left me alone, more or less. Because my mother had made it very clear that she did not approve. Nobody else did, but she kept a nice amount of money in the bank. And Mr. Porter always hired her. And he knew how my mother felt. And I imagine he conveyed it. Leave them alone. Stay away from the mains, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would she do? She hit kids?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, she just, like, beat them to pieces. Her favorite thing was switching a kid or beating them. Up in front of all the others, it was horrible. And I have known her saying, I had a headache yesterday. Because there was so much noise in the room. And we were third and fourth graders. Well, she's just going down there, but she never hit me. She knew that my mother had her license, if necessary. She would have fought it too. Because my mother said she is not an incorrigible child, and that is not necessary. Well, she keeps her, then put her with the older children. Don't have her with those little children. And they all knew. But, well, the other parents didn't have the nerve, my mother did. So their kids sometimes got in trouble. And I know, talking about later with Mary Adams. Mary, well, she lived in Moscow later. But Mary, she was an, she was a Johnson girl. One of her sisters married the Alexander that ran the store there. And her brother, her husband's sister was Mrs. Porter. She, Mrs. Porter was an Adams. So, but Mary was a teacher in a very different chart. And she taught most of her life. She said, I used to just have a terrible time having her next door. I hear these things going on and I couldn't do anything about it, you know. And, oh, she was, she, but not her daughters. One of her daughters was one of the nicest teachers I ever had. Really lovely person. But, oh, I always called her the old Minnie Green. And she was so horrible to teach children. And I know one, they had a nice home. They lived later in the foster board. And T .O. was kind of nice. He sold subscriptions to the paper. But T .O. was, I don't think he crossed her right much. I think everything had to be done just by the Mrs. Green's wife. And, but my mother had let her know in very uncertain terms, directly to, to leave me alone. So she never bothered me. I was never hit with a stick. But, she was a familiar. And a real nervous rat. You know when she teaches children that. It's not necessary, I taught school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she stay there for many years?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh yes. It's too bad. Yes, it was horrible.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It was horrible, but she always had a job. Because her money went in the bank.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well I can understand where they might clash if they were the two big men in town.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: E .W. Oh yes, they were very different. They probably clashed.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
