words: I don't know about it, but they could have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Foster fairly well to do from his practice?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He has a very good income.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't think in the end he had anything. I think he was broke. But he had at that time a very good income. I think it was easy come and easy go. I think he was very generous. I think he gave it away. They always said he would, if you had money enough to pay, he might charge you quite a bit. If you didn't have any money, he might treat you anyway. And sometimes if you were running out of money and people that would say, well I've got to go. And you're not well yet, you're going to stay. I have no more money, but he paid the board to keep me in town to stay. He was that kind. He believed in himself. He was not the quack in the sense that some are. He didn't keep something around in the bottle and then show it to everybody and say this is what I removed. He wasn't that kind. He was not the... It made a difference between him and the quack. And as I say, he did have apparently a certain power and suggestion. And of course they said, well Dr. Foster -Drunk is worth more than two other doctors sober sometimes. Well that was debatable. But then he did have a certain thing.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't think anybody knew him that didn't... really knew him. That didn't have a respect for him, you know. And could be very aggravating I think. I think he would know how to be. Very aggravating if he wanted to be. But kind of a nice man, my way. Too bad. Tragic. He never got over legal staff. Never. From then on it was very downhill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that he was the key to Juliaetta's economy?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: At that time he was the key.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, they had two hotels that did a good business. They had a lot of rooming houses. People had borders, could sign borders. They made some money. For sure. And he was gone. Didn't have anything. Just nothing. Once in a blue moon somebody at the hotel. I mean, you know, if you drive them in, yes. But nothing really. Keep one hotel that you count as a middle existence, but that was all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That building that he used to have, was that really almost like a hospital that he had there? Or was it just a big old office building?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, more of an office. More of an office. I don't really remember. I can't quite remember where it was, where we were taken. I remember where he lived, but I remember his office building in Clarkston. I thought it was a building with a balcony on it on Main Street. It might have been, but I don't really remember. I don't, I know I was taken there, but I was only a child. Five, I think, or something like that. It's kind of a, kind of a doctor. I'd go, I don't think it would have been good at that time. There wasn't anything that much wrong with me either. But my mother had the idea that that was the thing to do. And I know my best friend, her father, always believed in all those sort of things. And he believed in, very much in spiritualism too. And we said we, neither one of us want anything to do with that kind of, with any of the so -called rub doctors. We had too much of it as kids, you know, as parents that had faith in us. And we just never did. It was never the thing with us. Her mother didn't either so much. And she wouldn't believe in the spiritualism. That was the works of the devil, so keep it outside. But he thoroughly believed in that. I don't know whether Foster did that or not.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, I understand that there was a fairly strong strain of spiritualism in Juliet in the early days. Well, it might have been.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Now he may have been just close a little bit to them. I don't know whether he was or not. I remember him that way. I don't remember that either. But the Sniders, you know, they were the original people. I'd heard that they were involved in a group of spiritualists.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They used to believe that there was coal on the hillside. They'd quite dig out and they had some visions about it.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Now, I don't know. Now, the Sniders, of course I knew them, you know, but I don't know that about them. And then the Shuffers, they were the Sniders and the Shuffers from the people. Alexander Storr and Adams that built the castle, as it were, that's up there. And the Taylors. And Mrs. Ottomans. Mrs. Knapp and the Bruel family. Let me see. The Irwins lived down the river and my mother knew them at Palouse. They were from Palouse, so she knew them from Palouse. And the Whalens and the Albrights. Those I remember very well, the names. I can look at a picture and not tell. Oh, there was the Pierce family and there was Dunlap. I can't begin. The Caukeys had the hotel. The Rose had the drugstore. And they moved to the Houston later. And I knew them as they grew up. And the Porters, and there were different Porter families. There was the... RH Porter. And Lester Porter and I chucked together by then. Now they lived on the Porter Triad trip, Porter Place. And the bankers daughter was a little younger. And the Langdons, it was Langdons. And of course the Martins had the mail.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And...

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: There was a Civil War veteran living up on the street that's just back of Main Street.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: What is that street?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It's Water Street below and the one just back of Main Street up the hill. And when World War I broke out, we marched around. When we got into World War I, the United States got in. And then school marched just around and we sang all our idea of patriotic songs. And I remember we went into this old soldier's house and sang. Probably around the flag boys I guess and a few more things like that. Battle Hymn of the Republic, no doubt. And that was of course the war, the Andal War. And then there was no flower. And no sugar. But we had plenty of it in the cannery. Because you had sugar in the cannery so there was no problem there. But cans were the problem. My father's hair turned several shades grayer with that worry. But sugar he could get. Flower, because he had to have flowers to make labels. Make labels for his fruit. So that was the sense there was plenty of it. He had it and Eben Anderson Adams came to it with Alexander's Thorn. He said, I don't know what I'm going to do. He said, my farmers you know can't get into town sometimes. The roads got so bad in those days. And he said they need to lay in their whole winter supply and they won't let me sell it to them but a month at a time. And so he dashed out to take care of it. He gave me the supplies in the cannery. And they pretended it was bushed out but he couldn't get it. He could get Carlota. So flower and sugar went up to the ridges from the cannery by Alexander's Thorn. That's nice. But the flower was used for making the labels on the can. That was the way he could get it. Well in World War II I could get sugar. I found finally quite a bit of it. Quite generously for... I used it. I did use it. And a spray that we used on lads at the time of the trip. We didn't have... One came out later that was a different one. But that time we mixed a certain poison with the syrup as a base. So I marched up to the ration board and showed them that. And I wondered how much I would get. And I thought, boy I'm going to use some of this in my cans. It doesn't have any sugar.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And he said, well will 20 pounds help you?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I said, it's surely well. He said, come back when you need some more. Because I was about to suck again. Sugar was black market otherwise no way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you using it for?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well I couldn't use it in a spray for a syrup. I'm glad. A syrup, yeah. But I didn't use all of it there. Oh well, I wouldn't either. Oh but that was why I could get sugar. And otherwise you couldn't get sugar. I couldn't. I didn't have any way of getting butter. I didn't have any way of getting more meat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really in World War I? Was it really hard to find people?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, it was hard on flour and sugar in World War I. Now meat wasn't a problem I don't think. But flour was. You had to use so much rye and so much barley and so much this and so much that to get a little bit of white. And mostly the breads were brother's sad affairs. And sugar was scarce. But as I say because we had plenty of sugar in the cannery we didn't feel that at all. We had carton of sugar and that was it. So we had all the sugar we wanted and we could have all the flour we wanted. The mother did try to make some of the dark breads. They wasn't very good. And we bought a lot of bread.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But that was a hardship for a lot of the flour and the sugar. And then in World War II you see it was sugar and the canned stuff and the meats. That was where it hurt there. The meat. And I know my mother said, now this was with Virginia Sher, the little girl that she took to Rainsville growing up. And they said, she said, well I don't think this is fair. Duration the meat at home but you can go out and buy it. Only one day, there was one meatless day at the restaurant. I think it was Thursday. And they didn't expect anybody else to eat. But any other time you could go any longer and you had the fries, you could have your steak or your roast or anything else. I said, well it just leaves more for the rest. And mother said that isn't fair at all. I remembered my mother was right. But you could eat out and get what you didn't get at home. And I know there were certain things we just, well you just didn't have. Ham took a great deal of cheese. I forgot what it tasted like during World War II. And we loved cheese but it was high ratio. Took an awful lot. And of course some people with big families had more. But we had certain priorities on our family. We didn't reach to that. Father was very scarce. And we didn't have the good margarine. She could get margarine but it wasn't colored. It had a color. And we didn't like margarine. And some people didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could your father get the cans during World War I?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He lost a lot of fruit on the counter.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Normally, the Oregon packing company was in Lewiston and normally the two companies would either want to loan the other but they wouldn't loan anybody nothing. And we lost a lot of cherries and had a very bad lawsuit over it. And they dumped tons of cherries because they didn't arrive in time. And that was just railroad tie up. That was a car shortage as I recall. I suppose, railroad tie up. And the telegrams went and the telegrams went and my father's hair was rare and rare.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Adams and um...oh what was his name?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: My word, I can't forget that. He ran a store up there and my mother didn't know anyone to lose. Anderson. Anderson. Didn't have very much fruit. So they, the cannery always contracted for their fruit, the main orchards and cherries. And they had a lot of fruit ahead. Generally, the talks would be going on in the mid -winter, in January. They would be quite serious talks. What they would pay, I remember that. Imtern and some of the other big orchards would be in. And that would set the price. And of course, if you contracted for all your big orchards then no other buyer came in. And that you had that and they tried to do it because they needed that much in order to run it properly. And then no other buyer came in, but they bought everybody's fruit at that price. If you had two trees they didn't go through the form of a contract. But they bought your two trees approved at the same time. At the same price as the main contract. So it really was not a bad thing except this year when there was no cans. All right, they had to take the contract. They then get too ripe on the tree to wait because they keep looking. But then the men picked them and brought them in and hoped that tomorrow they'd be here in time to can them. And tomorrow they weren't. But the fellow with two trees came in with his trees. You weren't going to buy them. You weren't under contract to dump. So finally they left them at what they call grower's risk. And they wrote on the ticket, if the cans come. Well, the cans didn't come and they were dumped. So, Mr. Anderson said, it matters not to me what you did to them. I delivered them and I want my money. And he got Mr. Adams to take his check and finally decided not to go along. So they sued the can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who? Adams too?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Adams and Anderson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which Adams was that?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: So, Eben Adams' father and Mrs. Porter's father and all that. Well, his case was thrown out, but Anderson's because he hadn't accepted the check, went through. And so it was, the case was to come up in Moscow. And they debated about it. And they said morally if they had to pay Adams, which was just a small amount of turies, or if they had to pay Anderson, that they morally should pay everybody. And that would be quite a bit. And they debated whether they would do it or not. Because Mr. Justin was a very moral man and my father was a very upright man. And they decided they would find him. So, my folks, my father, Dean Tried in Moscow went to Mr. Moore, Frank Moore, because my mother had known Frank Moore in Palouse. And they were Democrats and he was a Democrat. And this was a Republican, Utah County. And the other went to Oberst Smith, who was the Republican lawyer. And, of course, my mother had known Mrs. Moore and she knew Frank Moore before they were married. So that was, I mean, she couldn't go to anybody but the Moore's because they were family. I called Mrs. Moore's sister, Annie, because we were close. They had heard Mrs. Moore's father had, Mrs. Moore's dad, a sister that had been a child of my mother's, who died. And, Annie, her husband had stayed at my grandmother's house at one time, boarded in another. Oh well, way back we just knew each other and they were devots from Palouse. So that was it. You had to go to Frank Moore. And Mr. Justin talked with some other lawyers, but Frank Moore would have to represent us up there. And they had the first hearing and political things being what they were, why we lost. So they went to the Circuit Court, the Red Deer Court. And there they had all the witnesses in the town, was very upset. Anderson with his and mine and the cannery were theirs. And that time they went with everybody and they traced the turies from the time they came into the cannery to the time they were dumped. The only one they didn't use was Anderson's son, who worked for my father and didn't get along with his father. And he offered to testify and Dad said, no, there's already too much bad blood, I'm not going to add to it. I'd prove it without you. So, he didn't take Lloyd, but everybody else was there. And we won the case. The turies were dumped.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, the fact is that you had written on the ticket that grower's risk.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But they said that wasn't legal. That wasn't enough. That wasn't legal was his argument. But, of course, he tried to say that they probably canned them and didn't pay the people. And, of course, they had these different people. And, naturally, Over Smith had to do something. So he picked, my father was always sorry, he brought one man as a witness. Because he was the only one who had a little bit questionable reputation. And, of course, to the jury he talked about that reprobate. And they always have to say something to a jury. And they picked a weak spot or a strong spot to do it. But, anyway, he lost the case. And he had attached a bunch of turies that were under order to the government. The government sent out a list at those times and watched your... Everything was sold before we canned it. You know, you had your orders. But they said, you've got so many cans for this and reserve this for the government. It doesn't make any difference about your contracts. That is the government. But it was reserved, but they hadn't taken them yet. And the government was pretty ornery about that. Because sometimes they didn't take. And then your other sale was gone. And the price went from something that was worth $8 a case was worth $1 .50. Well, you went broke sometimes. It's pretty hard in some of the canaries. Well, anyway, Anderson, when he filed the suit and he had got the paper served or something, he came down. Of course, my dad was Irish and mad as could be.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And the sheriff or somebody, the deputy said, well, how much difference between you?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And I think it was $2 and some odd cents. It was a very small amount.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For what?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: The difference in the price of paying him for his darn turies or not. You see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, Anderson's suit was over $2 .50?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: $0 .68, I think, or something like that. Yes, that was why he was so important. But, of course, as they said, they would have spent a lot more money involved. Because if he had had a right to that, everybody else would have. And it would have been them quite a bit more. But, still, that was the thing. And the man said, for heaven's sakes, and he was attaching oh so many cases of it.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: How much were they worth?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: My father told me how much they were worth. It was so ridiculous. This attachment, thousands of dollars worth of debt. Well, they stored it. Yes, I don't care what you do with it, but the government already has it out. It's going. You know that. Well, they stored it under his brother's name because he couldn't store it himself in his store. But, as I say, he was the dominant political party and he was a resident and we voted down here and we were Democrats. So, anyway, it was stored under his brother's name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's brother's name?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Anderson's brother. And, um, Nealey Anderson's husband. My mother always liked Nealey. Well, anyway, the darn thing burned and he said, true, it's his son. And he had to pay for it at the price it was. And he lost a lot of money. Anderson didn't help him any others either. He let his brother take the loss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Let his brother take the loss. He lost quite a bit. He had to pay for it at the price it was. And, burned and insurance had left.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, boy. Yeah.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: So, and, um, never as long as he lived did my father have a kind thought for Anderson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father and business didn't actually lose money on that fire then?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Nothing on the fire. He didn't lose a thing. But, he did lose on the turies they dumped. Because they had their big contract on turies and they dumped it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Anderson trying to do? I mean, for $2 .68?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He was, I don't know. He wanted to be the big man and fight the company. I guess. And it's the big company, the company, the big company. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much was, how much do you think at the time, Juliaetta sentiment was on Anderson's side?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, not at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not at all, huh?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They all understood. Well, their livelihood was at the camera. Their jobs, they worked for my father. And that, they knew one thing had happened there. And they knew that the thing had been done. They knew they lost. They knew one thing had happened. Oh, no. No, and they didn't like the other man very much anyway. He wasn't very popular. The old man, Hamill, they ran the hotel at that time. At Cowpiece and Soil and the old man, Hamill. Great Hazel Frost's father. And, um, he went over to get some eggs. You could buy at Anderson's store, Anderson's Rain Store, or at Alexander's. And, um, the hotel was up at the top of the grade, of Main Street, the corner. And then there was Anderson's store and Alexander's was over here and the bank was over here. Well, he had gone over to Anderson's to get some eggs with his basket. And he said, I don't want those small eggs. I want big eggs. And Anderson said, you've got to take them as they come. And he was taking them out this way because they weren't in cartons like we have now. And he said, I don't want those. I want the big ones. I care what it is. I want the big ones. Nope. Yeah, I dig it. Pretty soon Hamill says, I don't want your little eggs. And he took his basket and he slammed it in the whole case. And everybody's time just roared about them. But real badly he stalked out. And Anderson lost the hotel business and had a bunch of broken eggs. And that was the end of that. Because Hamill had just about as much temper as he did.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And Anderson was like that. He slammed it in the whole case of eggs.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, case of eggs. You know how egg cartons work. He just slammed his basket down in there. He wouldn't have his little eggs and out he stalked. And if you knew Mr. Hamill you could see him do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, had Anderson ever had run in with your father or the cannery prior to that?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No. No. They had been reasonably friendly. Maybe he didn't like, he didn't go along with the son that worked for my father. Maybe that had something to do with it. Could be. My mother knew him. They weren't close friends or anything like that. But they had no trouble. They knew each other from Palouse.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And he had no friends. Close friends. Exactly. He was a very ardent Republican.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your family become, be Democrats in the Republican Party?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, my father was a New York Irishman. You never were anything but a Democrat if you were that until the New Deal came along. Then he didn't like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't like the New Deal?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh no. He didn't like it at all. He didn't like Roosevelt. But up until then he was a good Democrat. Even probably, I don't know, they always voted in Eastern Oregon because sometimes it was much more economic for the middle Republicans. But he was one. That was a hard thing. Hard decision then. But he didn't like the New Deal and his sister had paid him a visit. And they hadn't seen each other for many, many years. And Dad could imitate Roosevelt perfectly. And he did something and she was furious she liked Roosevelt. I think if there had been a train down here at 10 o 'clock at night she'd have taken it. And Mother and I finally saved the day by laughing at him because it really was funny. Brother and Sister after all this time just quarrelling so. And she said, well, my heart, you're nothing but a Republican. And no word ever sounded worse than the way she said Republicans. Well, no, he wasn't the most built Democrat but he wouldn't have missed being a Republican because they were Democrats. Those New York Irish were Democrats. They were Democrats before they left the old country almost. It was in the family to be Democrats. And it was in my mother's idea that you were always the underdog. She always supported the underdog. The party that was out. Now, of course, I know she voted for Bert and Elle French, which was Republican. If she voted, she couldn't vote up there. She kept her vote down here but she would have voted for French, of course. I mean, but she'd always vote for the one that was out. You know, there was a small party as a rule. Usually speaking, that was, she would support that. But I think they had supported Brian, you know. And I don't know, I think my grandfather who read, my mother's father who read all the papers very thoroughly kept up. And he was inclined to be for the underdog, I think, a little bit. But for a European coming to this country, now he learned the language by reading the paper. He said he made the point to read every day the paper. And if he didn't know a word, he called it Jerusalem. And to begin with, there were quite a few Jerusalems. But he got, and he spoke with no accent except that he said he was a European. And my father had no Irish accent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father had actually come from Ireland?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, his folks had. But lots of that generation kept an accent. But he didn't think that was right at all. He despised people that kept an accent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what was that?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It was ridiculous, you know, to him. Oh no, no accent. It wasn't until I was East with him, I know that my, I met my generation, which was third generation Irish. And some of them spoke with an accent. He was very, very full of grace, the Lord given me. I had a father, you know.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I said, what? I said, what are they saying almost?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It just seemed funny to me. Because some do, there's some just, I think, I couldn't understand. My mother had kids, two boys, and their mother was a graduate of the university there in Syracuse. My father was a successful businessman. Yes, baby.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: How in the world?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You know, why they didn't get over the accent. Now, of course it's kind of fun to put it on, but to have it is a thing that you can't drop. It's kind of like a Southern accent.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You know, I mean, why keep it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm going to get going in a minute, Catherine, because I know we've been here for a long time. But I wanted, there's a story, there's one story I wanted you to tell me again. Because you mentioned it when we were talking, I didn't really get the whole story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that was about Jake, talking about Jake Rosenstein and his, you were saying that he used to have to travel around as a peddler?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He started out as a peddler, carried a pack on his back as a peddler. That was his beginning.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And he would have said he was a poor Jew. His sister had married a wealthier Jew and lived in Oakland. And he had a very wealthy brother in Roseburg. And they financed my grandfather in business. But Jake, of course, prospered in the end. He was very successful in Genesis.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a story you told me about him going to, it was a weigh station?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh yes, one of those terrible weigh stations sometimes where you had to stay. And this one was one of those places and there was no place for miles, you know. And he had come in with his, probably still with his pack, he was a peddling Jew. And they charged a big price for the room and the meal. And you probably had to take the two together. And I think the meal was costing him about a dollar. And you could get a good dinner for a quarter. And a very fine dinner for fifty cents. And there was nothing but ham and mustard, he said. And he told the man, he said, I don't eat ham, I'm Jewish. And he said, well help yourself to the mustard. And that was it. I know he told my grandfather that. And they had had these various experiences in traveling through where they had had hardships, you know. Oh yes. And anybody in the West, my father and my mother had them at various times. You might travel from one place to another and you see you travel with stagecoach and it was slow traveling. And one place you stop where they fed them and changed horses and they might be very nice. You might have a good bed, you might have warmth and good food. The next place, just terrible. My mother in Eastern Oregon, when she was sent in by the company, the telephone company, one place was, they got in about, oh she said four or five in the morning. It was very cold, they'd ridden all night and the stagecoach had no top on it. And the rain and the blanket that they had to cover himself was so heavy, it almost let it slide off and it was wet and she was so miserable and the mud had gotten in her face and she was in a mess. And she was so cold that she said, well I don't want a room, I just want to get warm by the stove. Because they would be leaving and they said, we put out the fire, you have to take a room. Well it didn't make any difference in money to her because the company was paying for it. But she had to take a room. And so she said she didn't undress at all and it was a good thing. There were kind of horse blankets on the bed and then the next morning there were some very badly burned biscuits and that was all. It was a dollar for the meal. And she said, oh there was some side pork, it was something you couldn't eat, you know, nothing else. It was just terrible coffee that was undrinkable. And my father, if they could pair notes, had been going through on the same route. And I think either before or just after. And he heard them, they pulled the same stunt on him and he heard the girls snickering about. He said, I wonder if anybody would think if he knew that men before him had smallpox in there. But neither one of them caught smallpox and they weren't vaccinated for it either. But I think it was because they didn't undress, you know, they were so cold. They were so miserable that they just didn't take off their clothes and the horse blankets weren't very attractive. And maybe they had something else around them too, everything, their own personal things. So they evidently didn't catch it. And then the next place might be very nice and feed you well. But you were at the mercy in traveling in these weigh stations and sometimes they were pretty lawful. And it was slow travel, you know. And the stagecoach, horse and buggy and mud and bad weather and over almost non -existent mountain roads. 200 miles to your nearest depot, you know, freight train. And it was rugged in that.
